followup_notes.changes_from_prior_cycle: No prior cycle; this is the initial review.

main_claims.claim: The right Rindler wedge alone supplies a globally hyperbolic spacetime with constant-Rindler-time Cauchy slices on which the coupled system can be evolved.
main_claims.notes: This is a standard property of the Rindler wedge, and the paper uses it in a limited way.
main_claims.paper_location: Sections I, IV; especially lines around the paragraph before Eq. (6)

main_claims.claim: The ambient field state induced in the right wedge by the Minkowski vacuum is the thermal Rindler state written in Eq. (5).
main_claims.notes: The statement is standard as a KMS/quasi-free state, but the displayed density matrix over continuum modes is formal and would need a regulator or algebraic-state interpretation for a fully mathematical presentation.

main_claims.claim: Second-order perturbation theory with the interaction Hamiltonian Eq. (6) gives the de-excitation probability Eq. (11).
main_claims.notes: The selection of the U1 rho_i U1^dagger term and the thermal sums are locally auditable and appear consistent. The derivation suppresses domain, continuum-normalization, and convergence details.

main_claims.claim: Equation (11) agrees with Eq. (19) of the 2015 paper, so the earlier formula is not invalid merely because the original intermediate calculation used both Rindler wedges.
main_claims.notes: The wedge-only derivation supports the rebuttal, assuming the mode normalizations and gamma conventions exactly match the earlier paper. The causal discussion in Section VI is more heuristic than the calculation itself.
main_claims.paper_location: Section V after Eq. (13), and Section VI

missing_assumptions.why_it_matters: Without this bound the left cavity endpoint does not define a finite point in the right Rindler wedge, so the geometric setup and mode expansion are not well-defined as stated.

missing_assumptions.assumption: The thermal Rindler state in Eq. (5) is to be understood with a regulator or as a KMS state on wedge-local observables.
missing_assumptions.paper_location: Section III Eq. (5), Section V traces
missing_assumptions.why_it_matters: This prevents formal continuum products and traces from being mistaken for normal trace-class density matrices.

missing_assumptions.assumption: The coupling in Eq. (6) is the intended model Hamiltonian, with any metric factors absorbed or intentionally absent.
missing_assumptions.why_it_matters: Different choices of local coupling density change the overlap integrals gamma and therefore the numerical de-excitation probability.

missing_assumptions.assumption: The comparison with Eq. (19) of the 2015 paper uses the same mode normalization and the same definition of the barred/tilde gamma coefficient.
missing_assumptions.why_it_matters: The paper's central claim is equality with the earlier formula; notation drift could otherwise hide a substantive difference.

needs_human_spotcheck.question: Does Eq. (11) exactly match Eq. (19) of Lorek-Louko-Dragan 2015 under the same normalization conventions?
needs_human_spotcheck.why_model_cannot_resolve: The reply asserts the equality, but a fully reliable check requires comparing the original paper's notation and normalizations in detail.

needs_human_spotcheck.question: Is the dxi interaction Hamiltonian in Eq. (6) exactly the interaction used in the original model, or should a metric/lapse factor appear for the intended physical coupling?
needs_human_spotcheck.why_model_cannot_resolve: This depends on the modeling convention in the 2015 paper and on whether the authors intend a phenomenological Hamiltonian or a covariant interaction action.

needs_human_spotcheck.question: Are there infrared or near-horizon convergence issues in the Omega integral in Eq. (11), especially near Omega = 0?
needs_human_spotcheck.why_model_cannot_resolve: Resolving this needs detailed asymptotics of the specific Rindler modes U_Omega and the finite-time overlap integrals.

novelty_assessment: The paper is not a broadly novel mathematics or QFT contribution; it is a concise clarification of a specific published objection. Its value is in giving a cleaner wedge-local derivation of an existing formula and thereby separating the physical term in Eq. (11) from the alleged cross-wedge artifact.
score_rationale: The central wedge-only perturbative derivation looks plausible and is useful as a reply, but the paper is short and formal, with several implicit hypotheses that matter for mathematical rigor. The contribution is mainly corrective/clarifying rather than novel, so I rate it as solid but routine rather than field-shaping.
summary: The note gives a narrow reply to a published Comment: it redoes the de-excitation calculation using only the right Rindler wedge, with the ambient field initialized in the wedge restriction of the Minkowski vacuum, and recovers the earlier probability formula. The local perturbative algebra is plausible, but several hypotheses are left implicit, especially the cavity-size restriction and the formal use of a continuum thermal density matrix.

technical_objections.evidence: The paper states only l > 0. If l >= 2/alpha, xi_- is undefined or at the horizon/outside the wedge, contradicting the assumed right-wedge cavity setup.
technical_objections.objection: The allowed range of the cavity length is not stated. The definitions xi_pm = alpha^{-1} log(alpha sigma_pm) with sigma_pm = alpha^{-1} +/- l/2 require sigma_- > 0, i.e. l < 2/alpha, for both walls to lie in the right Rindler wedge.
technical_objections.paper_location: Section II, cavity definition before Eq. (3)

technical_objections.evidence: The calculation writes a product over all Omega and sums over |n>_Omega, then uses Tr over the full Hilbert space. A rigorous version should specify box normalization, wave packets, or a KMS two-point-function formulation.
technical_objections.objection: Eq. (5) is used as an ordinary density matrix and then traced over continuum Rindler modes, but the continuum tensor product and mode-number states are distributional/formal.
technical_objections.paper_location: Section III Eq. (5), Section V Eq. (10)-(11)

technical_objections.evidence: For a covariant volume coupling in the Rindler metric one would expect lapse/volume factors unless absorbed into lambda or the field definitions. The formula is fine as a definition of the model, but the paper should make this explicit because it affects Eq. (12).
technical_objections.objection: The interaction Hamiltonian is postulated with measure dxi, without spelling out whether this is a model definition or derived from a covariant local interaction density.
technical_objections.paper_location: Section IV, Eq. (6), and definitions of gamma in Eq. (12)

technical_objections.evidence: The paper says evolution within the cavity is independent of how constant-time slices are continued outside the cavity. This is plausible for local relativistic interactions, but boundary and foliation-independence assumptions are not stated or checked.
technical_objections.objection: The Section VI justification of the original two-wedge calculation appeals to deforming time slices outside the cavity but does not give the microcausality or Tomonaga-Schwinger argument needed to make this independence precise.
